# Hypersquare Geometry {#sec-hypersquares}

::: {.content-hidden}
{{< include ../_macros.qmd >}}
:::

Having established the classical framework in @sec-polytopes, we now consider a modification in which the ambient space is no longer assumed to carry a Riemannian metric. A *hypersquare* will refer, informally, to an $n$-cube equipped with a combinatorial structure that replaces the usual smooth notions of distance and curvature.

::: {#def-hypersquare}
An $n$-dimensional *hypersquare* consists of a combinatorial $n$-cube $Q$ together with:

1. An assignment of a transport operator $T_e$ to each oriented edge $e$ of $Q$, and  
2. A coherence condition on each 2-face requiring that the composition of transport operators around the boundary of the face is equal to a fixed face-dependent automorphism.
:::

Instead of a metric tensor, we therefore work with these discrete transport operators and their associated coherence data. This allows us to speak of parallel transport and holonomy without requiring differentiability.

## Fundamental Phenomena {#sec-hypersquares-fundamental-phenomena}

One immediate consequence of this weakened structure is that standard metric identities hold only locally, if at all. For example, while @thm-pythagoras continues to describe distances within individual faces that admit Euclidean charts, there is no guarantee that these local computations are compatible when extended across the entire hypersquare. As a result, geodesic paths---defined here as sequences of edges minimizing a suitable discrete action---need not be unique, and may exhibit periodicity or self-intersection without degeneracy. These phenomena will form the basis for later constructions, where hypersquares serve as model spaces for more general non-metric topological objects.
